claim: The Voynich text uses a homophonic substitution cipher on medieval Latin or Italian, where 2-4 glyphs map to each high-frequency plaintext letter, reducing glyph entropy below what a simple substitution would produce (~4.0 bits) and inflating the hapax ratio by producing artificial variant spellings of the same underlying word.
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is below Latin baseline (~4.0) and Italian baseline (~3.95), consistent with homophone pooling reducing per-symbol information
Hapax ratio 70.1% is far above natural language norms (~40-50%), consistent with multiple cipher-surface forms mapping to the same plaintext word
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is slightly shallow compared to natural language (~1.0), consistent with artificial vocabulary inflation via homophones
High-confidence belief [0.286] already supports homophonic encoding in zodiac/label sections
evidence_against: Only 25 unique glyphs is a small alphabet for a homophonic system, which typically requires 50+ symbols for adequate coverage of Latin
Word entropy 10.4508 bits is high, suggesting a large effective vocabulary rather than a compressed homophonic map
If homophones were abundant, frequency distribution of glyphs should show clustering into groups â€” this has not been definitively demonstrated
test: For each pair of glyphs, compute their positional co-occurrence profiles (probability distribution over word position 1..N). Cluster glyphs by Jensen-Shannon divergence of their positional profiles. If homophones exist, clusters of 2-4 glyphs with near-identical positional profiles should emerge, each cluster representing one plaintext letter.
test_metric: Number of glyph clusters with intra-cluster Jensen-Shannon divergence < 0.05 bits and inter-cluster divergence > 0.3 bits
test_threshold: If 6 or more such clusters are found covering at least 15 of the 25 glyphs, homophonic substitution is supported; if fewer than 3 clusters, refute

reasoning: Homophonic ciphers were well-known in 15th-century Europe and are performable by hand. This hypothesis simultaneously explains below-baseline glyph entropy, anomalously high hapax ratio, and the moderate Zipf fit, making it one of the highest-leverage hypotheses to confirm or deny.
